---
title: ""
---

## Blog

- 2026/05/28. Read this [very cool paper](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2023.0661 "Shiratsuru et al. 2023") by Shiratsuru *et al.* 2023. the other day. There is a common assumption in the behavioural ecology of predator-prey relationships that prey would become *less active* (for what it may mean) at moments of the day when predators are *more active* - because predation pressure would be higher then. In this study, authors actually attempted to evaluate this - they measured predation rates all along the day and related it to predator *and* prey activity patterns (using a classic lynx-snowshoe hare system). What they found is that kills are mainly related to predator activity, while they were completely unrelated to the prey - see the cool figure below. The result is somewhat similar to [a previous one](doi:10.1111/1365-2435.12642 "Vander Vennen et al. 2016") obtained on a wolf-moose system. Cool to see some papers that actually do test common assumption that is often invoked but actually rarely tested - although often they are at the core of causal rationales. Anyway, I'll keep this in mind for my future investigations 🙂

  - [![Diel activity of lynxes (blue dashed line), snowshoe hares (green dashed), and predation events (red solid). Fig. 1c from Shiratsuru et al. 2023.](/img/shiratsuru_activity.png)](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2023.0661)

- **2026/05/26.** Another thought-provoking quote today by Adam Mastroianni on [Experimental History](https://www.experimental-history.com/p/shame-them-shun-them-ban-them-beat).

  ::: {}
  > You can’t turn a cheat into a scientist by making a rule against cheating. The most important “rigor-enhancing practice” is caring about getting things right, and without that, nothing else matters.
  :::

  What are your thoughts on this? Part of me agrees. Sure, things seem more right if people *do* adhere to the open/reliable science principles rather than if they are forced to. But how do we *make them adhere* to these principles? *Should we*? Maybe the question is rather: how de we counter other incentives that would make them adopt *bad practices*?

- **2026/05/21.** Just saw this citation by Scott Peacor today on [Dynamic Ecology blog](https://dynamicecology.wordpress.com/2026/05/18/designing-a-statistic-project-to-show-that-we-ecologists-are-doing-something-wrong-is-not-my-idea-of-fun-interview-with-scott-peacor-on-meta-analysis-in-ecology/) today.

  ::: {}
  > I want my work to have a positive influence, not just have fun. This is not fun for me. I don’t really like statistics and I don’t like criticizing people. I don’t get a kick out of it. And so designing a statistic project to show that we ecologists are doing something wrong is not my idea of fun.
  :::

  Found it striking (and inspirational)? Reminds me the [famous citation from Richard McElreath](https://elevanth.org/blog/2025/07/09/which-kind-of-science-reform/): 'Research is a job, not a hobby' (famous at least for me, haha). WHat do you think?
